title: Fund Balance Strategy, ARPA Spending Cliff, and Post-Federal Revenue Outlook
summary: Revenue surplus driven by ~$8M in ARPA funds (difficult to budget); expenditure savings from unfilled positions across multiple departments plus DPW equipment maintenance (~$1M) and assessment department (~$1M, due to reval not occurring). ARPA funding ($80M+ total) now essentially exhausted -- programs previously supported must find replacement funding. HUD program future uncertain. State pass-through funding expected to decline as federal flows decrease. Sales tax starting to decline ('recession indicator'). Interest income expected to tail off with falling rates. Fund balance discussion: $114M unassigned at ~50% of annual expenditures; S&P and Moody's credit rating partially tied to high balance. Auditor recommends fund balance plan: use surplus for one-time projects and capital expenditures, fund ongoing operations with stable revenue (property taxes); spread drawdown over 5-year plan; warns against supporting ongoing programs with fund balance. 'Evan' (administration finance staff) noted in audience. Council asks Bonadio for ARPA spending breakdown by program -- auditors can provide generalizations (mix of revenue replacement/salaries and project-based spending). State OSC could criticize high fund balance levels, though all municipalities similarly positioned post-ARPA.

political_signal.description: Council pressing on ARPA cliff implications, fund balance deployment strategy, and whether budget is realistic given declining revenue sources

processing_notes: IPS in detected_entities is false positive; excluded
DPW and DPD in detected_departments are referenced as budget categories with surpluses, not acting departments; Finance is primary
Assessment reval not happening = reason for ~$1M surplus in assessment department budget
'Evan' in back of room = likely administration finance staff; not identified further
ARPA = American Rescue Plan Act; $80M+ total received by city, now essentially spent
Fund balance level of 50% of expenditures is historically unprecedented for the city
